items.q: What did Rutherford's alpha-scattering experiment show about the atom?
items.correct: Most of the atom is empty space, with a tiny dense positive nucleus.
items.misconception: It showed that atoms are solid spheres of positive charge with electrons embedded inside.
items.random_wrong: It showed that alpha particles can pass through any material because they are massless.
items.why: Students confuse the plum-pudding model (which Rutherford disproved) with the conclusion of his experiment.

items.q: What is the difference between heat and temperature?
items.correct: Heat is the energy transferred due to temperature difference; temperature measures average kinetic energy of particles.
items.misconception: Heat and temperature are the same thing — both measure how hot something is.
items.random_wrong: Heat is measured in degrees Celsius and temperature in Joules.
items.why: Everyday language uses 'heat' and 'temperature' interchangeably ('turn up the heat'), so students don't distinguish them physically.

items.q: Why is nuclear fusion much harder to achieve on Earth than fission?
items.correct: Fusion requires extremely high temperatures and pressures to overcome the electrostatic repulsion between positive nuclei.
items.misconception: Fusion is harder because the materials are rarer than uranium.
items.random_wrong: Fusion is harder because you need to break atoms apart first.
items.why: Students don't appreciate the Coulomb barrier; they assume the challenge is technological rather than fundamental physics.

items.q: Energy to heat 1 kg water by 10 C (J):
items.why: Correct answer: 42000. “420” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Energy to vaporise 1 kg water (J) approx:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2260000. “334000” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Mass 600 g, volume 200 cm^3. Density in g/cm^3?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
